data.title: Travelzoo: Improved Earnings On Seasonally Cheaper CAC, But Its Economics Don't Seem Attractive
data.description:
Travelzoo (TZOO) recently reported 1Q26 earnings.

The stock jumped significantly after the release, I would believe primarily on the sequential improvement in operating income. This change in results probably stems from lower advertising expenses, which, according to the company, are a source of profits down the road.

The results do not solve a concern expressed in previous quarters, however, about whether the economics of the new membership model, with high CACs, work at all, despite already being more than a year into the program.

With the additional risks to international travel posed by the conflict in the Middle East, I believe the stock is unattractive at these prices, which are higher than when I last rated a stock a Hold. Therefore, I maintain a Hold rating on TZOO.

1Q26 Results

The main data point of the earnings release is the company posting operating income of $3.4 million for the quarter. This was below the profits last year ($3.8 million, $4.4 million on a non-GAAP basis), despite higher revenues, implying lower margins, but they were a significant improvement on a sequential basis.

The main difference with previous quarters, like 4Q25, is a decrease in G&A (positive because G&A is not a value-generating activity for the customer) and lower relative S&M.

The lower relative S&M might be explained by the lower CAC. During the call, the company said that "average acquisition cost per club member was $28 in Q1 2025, $38 in Q2, $40 in Q3, $34 in Q4, and $27 in Q1 2026." This is probably the result of seasonality, as targeting travel-oriented customers online is probably cheaper during the travel offseason in Q4 and Q1 and more expensive during spring-summer (Q2 and Q3).

Revenues continued to grow, but the question of the higher revenues is whether they are profitable. Advertising revenues stemming from the legacy business were flat YoY. The revenues that grew came from memberships.

As done in previous quarters, we can estimate the net gain in members by assuming that the net increase in membership revenues ($500 thousand) is divided across $10 or $12.5 (the company recently raised prices from $40 to $50 per year in the US) of the first quarter accrual recognition of a new member. This indicates about 40 or 50 thousand net new members. The change in deferred revenues of $1.2 million also points to 40 thousand new members if we assume that the deferred figures are $30 per net new user.

The problem is that if the company wanted to acquire 40 or 50 thousand new members in Q2 or Q3, it would need to pay 40% to 50% more than in Q1 (based on the above disclosure). Given that it spent $2 million in Q1 (earnings presentation page 7), it will need to spend about $3 million in Q2 and Q3. This will reduce the profitability of the company back to 2Q25 levels (around $2 million).

Furthermore, the $2 million figure for aggregate customer acquisition costs is interesting. The company spent that figure and gained between 40 and 50 thousand net new members, who paid each $40 to $50. That is, the company spent $2 million (without considering overhead or others) to generate $1.6 to $2.5 million in future revenues. Again, the economics are not as good as the company shows with the CAC figure alone.

Furthermore, if the company spent $2 million at a CAC of $27, then it actually registered 74 thousand members but lost between 24 and 34 thousand to reach a net 40 to 50 thousand. The churned members have to have joined in 1Q25. At the time, the company had added 80 thousand net new members ($800 thousand difference in membership revenues between 1Q25 and 4Q24, as shown on page 6 of the earnings presentation 1Q25). This could imply a churn rate of as much as 40% per year, although it is potentially lower because the gross added members are probably more than 80 thousand (we cannot know because the company did not report aggregate acquisition costs for this business).

At, say, 30% churn per year, to keep the customer file constant at 460 thousand (that is, recover 138 thousand members per year), at an average CAC per customer of $35 (average of the four quarters mentioned above), the company would need to spend almost $5 million per year in direct marketing costs, or close to 30% of the revenues generated by that same user base ($18.4 million per year at $40).

Assuming that the members generate a margin above marketing costs of 24%, just like the rest of the operation, they are likely only barely profitable or even unprofitable. The 24% results from removing the $2 million in direct marketing from the operating expenses + CoGS in Q1 ($20.7 million, that is $18.7 million) and dividing by 1Q26 revenues of $24.5 million to reach CoGS+OpEx/Revenues of ~76%, or a contribution of 24%.

Valuation

Today, the company trades at a market cap of $104 million.

Assuming that the economics I presented above are accurate (and I should warn that they are highly speculative but the best we can do with the data at hand), at a constant run rate (that is, without spending on adding new customers), the company might be able to generate $97 million in revenues, which, using a contribution of 24% before marketing expenses for the membership business, implies $23 million. From this, we have to remove $5 million in expected marketing costs to keep the membership business constant, or $17 million. After taxes, this becomes $12 million.

This might seem attractive, but it is based on relatively optimistic economics, like a relatively low churn assumption or consistent CAC costs that do not grow over time.

In terms of the quality of the underlying business, the fact that the advertising has been working for several years, even decades, is a sign that some find it valuable. Still, it is a business that has not been growing in years.

I have doubts about whether memberships will be as durable and profitable, which are part of my discussion about long-term economics, particularly regarding churn levels.

Finally, on the qualitative front, we should remember this is a travel business that is exposed to discretionary income cycles. We should also add the quite relevant risk of disruption in air travel and tourism caused by already close to 2 months of a blockade in the Persian Gulf, which is affecting jet fuel prices and jet fuel supplies, on top of the myriad impacts on discretionary income that a general supply shock will have on the global economy.

In my opinion, TZOO's current share price could be considered a fair valuation if one used the above assumptions around business economics. However, given the lack of consistency in improving economics and the challenges presented to global tourism activity in 2026, the name is not attractive at current prices. I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Inflation Coming?
data.description:
The Federal Reserve added $9.9 billion in securities to its securities in the past banking week.

This was the twenty-first banking week since the Fed decided to begin another round of quantitative easing.

In this twenty-one-week period, the Federal Reserve has added $168.7 billion to its portfolio of securities held outright. This is an average of $8.0 billion per week over this period of time.

In the eight weeks since the "war" in Iran began, the Federal Reserve has added $81.5 billion to its securities portfolio. This is an average of just over $10.0 billion per week over this period of time.

The Federal Reserve has added securities to its securities portfolio at a faster pace since the "war" began, but the increase per week has not seemed to be that much greater.

Conclusion: The Federal Reserve has added securities to its portfolio at a faster rate since the start of the "war" with Iran, but the weekly addition has not been that large. The "war" has not changed things that much.

Here is a picture of how the portfolio has risen during this time period.

The important thing is that President Trump has wanted the Federal Reserve to lower its policy rate of interest.

Adding securities to the securities portfolio does increase the reserves of the banking system.

However, while the Federal Reserve has been increasing the size of its securities portfolio, it has also been doing other things within its balance sheet to offset this increase.

As a consequence, the Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks have not changed much at all.

The Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks can be thought of as the "excess reserves" of the banking system.

Over the past twenty-one weeks, the "excess reserves" in the banking system have risen by $16.6 billion, which is almost nothing. The "total" Reserve Balances in the commercial banking system were $2.9 trillion on April 22, 2026.

It does not appear as if the commercial banking system has seen any real growth in "excess reserves" over this time period.

One could argue that as the "excess reserves" of the banking system rose...there would be pressure on the Fed's policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate...to fall.

The Federal Reserve seems to be managing the "excess reserves" of the banking system in such a way that there is little "market pressure" for the Federal Funds rate to fall.

Consequently, little has been done in this area since the start of December 2026. Only one rate reduction has taken place.

So, over the past 21 banking weeks (Wednesday to Wednesday), the Federal Reserve has moved to a policy of quantitative easing, but they have managed their balance sheet in such a way that Fed officials have only felt the need to lower the Fed's policy rate of interest one time during this period.

But the commercial banking system is "loaded" with reserves!!!

And the Federal Reserve is adding securities to its securities portfolio at a fairly rapid pace.

And, Chairman Jerome Powell's term as the Fed chairman is to last only a few more weeks.

However, his term in office still has several years remaining.

Will he resign?

Will he create a space for Kevin Warsh to become the next Chairman?

This still has to be worked out.

The interesting thing to me in what the Federal Reserve has been doing these past twenty-one weeks.

I could make an argument that Mr. Powell and the Fed have created a monetary policy that is very open to Mr. Warsh becoming the Chairman of the Board of Governors and having a policy of quantitative easing already in place that would allow Warsh to step up the quantitative easing and get to two or three reductions to the Federal Funds rate this year.

Coming in now, Mr. Warsh will not have to "reverse" the direction of monetary policy.

Earlier in November, he would have had to "reverse" the direction in order to move from quantitative tightening to quantitative easing.

This "reversal" has already been completed.

Mr. Warsh will have a straight shot to lower rates.

We'll see.

The only thing I caution on has to do with the value of the U.S. dollar.

The value of the U.S. dollar has fallen since Mr. Trump took office in January 2025.

The general feeling is that the United States is heading for a period of rising inflation due to many of the policies the federal government has advanced over the past year.

Further reducing the Fed's policy rate of interest two or three more times this year may not be seen as healthy.

The value of the U.S. dollar may continue to drop...and could drop by a serious amount.

Inflation may be a major problem over the next several years.

This I am not in favor of.

data.title: REX American Resources Offers Potential, But Uncertainty Is A Problem
data.description:
One company that has really exceeded my expectations in recent months has been REX American Resources Corporation (REX). Conceptually, it is an interesting business. I say that because it focuses mostly on the production and sale of ethanol that is used for fuel. It does have some other things going forward as well. But that comprises most of the value of the business at this time. To be clear, the company does have certain catalysts that could result in additional value in the long run. But in my last article about it, published in December of last year, I made the decision to downgrade it from a Buy to a Hold.

This was because, even though the company had a lot going for it, including a very strong balance sheet, industry conditions remained challenged, leading to weakness in ethanol pricing. Long term, I was optimistic about export growth and lower corn input costs. But based on how the stock was priced, I could not in good conscience maintain my bullish stance. The good news is that REX stock had outperformed the market while I was bullish. Shares had risen 103.3% since I originally rated it a Buy in December of 2022. That dwarfed the 69.2% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw.

But since downgrading it, the stock has risen even further. It's currently up 26.6%, which is well above the 3.8% rise that the S&P 500 saw. This is in spite of the fact that the company has historically seen a lot of volatility from operations and can be chalked up to optimism centered around some recent financial performance and expectations about what the future holds. However, I am not the kind of investor who is willing to write the check for a future that might fail to live up to expectations. Until I see concrete data that shows that the company will see a meaningful expansion in profitability, I think that being principled and maintaining my Hold rating is the right choice.

Digging Into REX American Resources

Purely from a fundamental standpoint, I would say that REX American Resources is doing fine, but not exceptional. The only new data that investors have access to since the time I last wrote about the business covers the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $158 million. That was actually down from the $158.2 million that the business reported a year earlier. But even though revenue decreased, profitability skyrocketed. Net income surged from $11.1 million to $43.7 million. Operating cash flow more than doubled from $25.1 million to $53.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a surge from $19.6 million to $45.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $14.9 million to $21.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. Revenue increased some, but the real benefit came on the bottom line for the company. Profits and cash flows increased almost across the board. Only EBITDA worsened on a year-over-year basis. There is certainly a lot to unpack here in order to understand what is going on. As I mentioned at the start of this article, the company is an ethanol-based business. And historically, ethanol has experienced a lot of volatility in pricing. For the final quarter of 2025, for instance, the average sales price for ethanol rose to $1.72 compared to the $1.65 that it was a year earlier, even as the firm saw a decline in production from 74.6 million gallons to 70.1 million gallons. The end result here, unfortunately, was a drop in ethanol revenue from $122.8 million to $120.7 million.

This was not the only area of weakness for the business. Dried distillers grains revenue for the firm ended up falling from $23.9 million to $22.3 million. This was in spite of the fact that the average selling price actually increased from $143.80 per ton to $147.30 per ton. And it was the result of a drop in overall shipments amounting to 8.9%. On the other hand, the company did see a nice jump in distillers corn oil revenue from $10.4 million to $13.6 million. In addition to enjoying a rise in production from 23.5 million pounds to 25.1 million pounds, it saw the average sales price climb from $0.44 per pound to $0.54 per pound. This is interesting when you consider what is going on with the industry right now.

Earlier this month, USDA projected that about 91.3 million acres worth of corn will be harvested this year. That's up substantially from the 83 million that was harvested last year. In addition to acreage rising, there is the belief that yield per harvested acre will grow as well, climbing from 179.3 bushels per acre to 186.5 bushels. This translates to a 14.3% jump in overall production. And the belief is that the average price will fall from $4.24 per bushel to $4.15. I know that doesn't sound like much. But for the farming industry that has already been severely constrained in recent years, every little bit has an impact.

Even though lower pricing means lower revenue, all else being the same, it does not necessarily mean lower profits for a business such as REX American Resources. Around 80% of ethanol production costs involve corn. So as these input costs decline, there is the opportunity for margin expansion. Management specifically mentioned in their latest earnings call that reduced corn costs helped the company out. But obviously, the higher pricing that I detailed earlier also played a role here. In the chart below, you can see that, on a four-week average basis, U.S. exports of fuel ethanol continue to rise. This certainly bodes well for pricing even if overall ethanol production in the U.S. barely budges higher.

Another positive for the company, and perhaps the most significant one for the quarter, ended up being the 45Z tax credits that it received as a reduction to income tax expense. This is a fascinating program that was created under the Inflation Reduction Act and that extends from 2025 through 2029. Under this, different fuels get a carbon intensity score that measures total emissions. The lower this intensity score is, the higher the tax credit. And in some instances, when the carbon intensity score is high, a company might get no tax credit at all. Ethanol producers such as REX American Resources stand to benefit from this. Management actually believes that, at peak, it could get up to $150 million worth of tax benefits each year after the successful launch of its carbon sequestration assets. This is in addition to a credit of $85 per ton of carbon sequestered under the 45Q program that management believes could bring in additional tax credit benefits of around $36 million a year.

According to the company, during the final quarter of 2025, it recognized $28.1 million of tax credits under the 45Z program. This is significant in and of itself, and it was the main driver of profit growth during the quarter and the year. For 2026, there is the expectation that the business will continue to benefit from this program. However, there are other things that need to be taken into consideration. Most notably, we have its One Earth Energy ethanol production expansion and carbon capture projects. Management mentioned in their latest earnings release that they are nearing completion of the expansion of ethanol production at their One Earth facility, and they expect it to be fully operational sometime this year.

This is great, but there has been a bit of a setback when it comes to the company’s carbon capture and sequestration project. Even though management has done the work to get that project to fruition, the EPA timeline on approval has shifted. It was previously thought that it would be in June of this year. But now, that timeline has been moved to September. Because of this, for 2026 specifically, management does not expect any tax benefit associated with its carbon capture and sequestration activities. Only next year is that their hope that this will change.

Because of how volatile the picture is in this space, it's difficult to rely too much on financial performance data. And management sadly does not provide detailed guidance for investors. If we were to value the business based on results from the last two fiscal years, however, shares aren't exactly cheap. In fact, I would say that the business is somewhere between fairly valued and a bit overpriced. In the chart above, you can see exactly how the stock is valued at the moment. The EV to EBITDA multiple is especially unpleasant. But considering the opportunities that exist for the firm, I don't think that adopting a bearish outlook is necessary.

This is in part because, as the company completes the aforementioned projects and enjoys potentially large tax credits, it could see a nice boost in profitability. If we were to apply the maximum tax credits from both programs to the net income that the business generated in 2025, this would lower its price-to-earnings multiple for that year from 17.3 to just 6. But at this point, absent more concrete guidance from management, this is speculative and represents what I would consider to be the best-case scenario. Plus, it relies on a timeline that is highly uncertain and that wouldn’t yield this best-case scenario until at least next year. As a value investor, reliability is imperative. And if there is uncertainty that I am banking on, the discount already has to be baked in and clearly visible.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that exists today, I would argue that while I clearly downgraded the stock prematurely, the picture for the business does not justify upgrading it back to a Buy. The company does have certain catalysts that could push revenue and profitability higher this year and in future years. But because there is a lot of uncertainty over what that situation will look like, I am not ready to bank on it. Instead, using the data that we do have available, I believe that the firm is appropriately priced, and, as a result, I would argue that maintaining it as a Hold is prudent.

data.title: Chewy: Durable Demand And Consistent Earnings Growth
data.description:
Though the S&P 500 has easily ratcheted back to near all-time highs despite uncertainty over the conflict in Iran, many small- and mid-cap stocks, particularly in the tech space, remain in a deep bear market. Gains in the market have largely been concentrated toward chip stocks and energy names that have benefited from the datacenter boom and rising oil prices, respectively. In my view, investors wanting to bet on a return of the "risk on" trade and dispersion should take advantage of recent declines to pick up secular winners at a great price.

Chewy, Inc. (CHWY), the pet e-commerce company, is a fantastic candidate in this regard. The stock is down more than 20% since the start of January, and it's also sitting at nearly half of 52-week highs in the high $40s notched last summer. Meanwhile, the stock has rallied gradually after a recent late March Q4 earnings print, which, in my view, could be the beginning of a sustained rebound in this stock.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Chewy in December, when the stock was trading at $35 per share. At the time, I had pointed to the company's macro-defying strong growth rates, driven in particular by larger and larger average order sizes from a consistently expanding customer base. I see the same trends playing out in 2026, especially as the company continues to build up its gross margin base. I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion on Chewy.

For investors who are newer to Chewy, here is what I view to be the long-term bull case on the company:

  • Consistent demand trends and macro resilience. Amid a broader pullback in consumer spending, Chewy has maintained consistent, high single-digit growth rates, with the company continuing to add new active shoppers and increase average basket sizes.
  • Large percentage of autoship sales. In Chewy's most recent quarter, 84% of its revenue was derived from autoship orders, up from 81% in the prior-year quarter. The fact that most customers just order their goods from Chewy on repeat further adds to the stability of its top line.
  • Pet household formation growth. While we're no longer in pandemic-era levels of pet household formation, Chewy notes that its industry backdrop is favorable, with the company expecting low single-digit growth in pet spending (with dogs skewing toward the lower end of that forecast and cats at the higher end). Chewy's high single-digit growth implies share gains.
  • Further margin drivers in proprietary brands and vet care. The company's rising sales mix of store-owned brands, plus its expansion of its nascent vet care practice (now with 18 locations in 5 states), are further drivers to bolster the company's margin profile. The company's recent acquisition of Modern Animal nearly triples the scale of its vet business.

Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Chewy's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary (ending on February 1) is shown below:

Chewy's revenue was flat y/y at $3.26 billion, but note that the company faced a tough comparison versus a 14-week quarter in FY24. Adjusting for the fourteenth week, Chewy notes that its revenue growth would have been 8.1% y/y, which is sequentially consistent against 8.3% y/y growth in Q3. Again, this is a strong indicator that Chewy is resilient in the wake of a shakier consumer economy.

Meanwhile, autoship sales grew at an even faster 13% y/y pace to $2.74 billion, or 84% of revenue (versus 81% in the year-ago quarter). In my view, the increasing proportion of automatic orders is a core driver in Chewy being able to re-rate its stock upward, since its business is largely built on recurring orders.

The company expects to continue growing active customers at a healthy pace while beating overall pet industry trends in 2026. Per CEO Sumit Singh's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

We expect 2026 pet industry dynamics to largely mirror 2025, steady and resilient to macro trends, but without cyclical acceleration. Pet household formation appears stable with no evidence of deterioration. However, we are not underwriting a meaningful rebound in that variable.

Current estimates suggest low single-digit industry growth with dog at the lower end of that range and cat at the higher end. Further, we expect industry growth to be predominantly volume driven, with little or no contribution from pricing. Importantly, we expect a secular shift towards e-commerce penetration to continue as consumers increasingly prioritize convenience, transparency and auto replenishment, structural advantages that persist across economic environments and benefit scaled digital platforms like Chewy.

Against this backdrop, we once again expect to deliver share gaining growth.

On the profitability front, we note that Chewy's gross margins rose 90 bps y/y to 29.4%:

The company attributed the rise to an increased contribution from sponsored ads as well as health/wellness categories and a favorable sales mix into higher-margin products. We note that many retail companies have reported weakening gross margins on account of higher promotional activity to draw in sales in this macro environment, something which Chewy isn't doing.

Adjusted EBITDA grew 38% y/y to $162.3 million, with the company achieving 30 bps of opex leverage on top of gross margin gains to hit 5.0% in adjusted EBITDA margins, up 120 bps y/y:

Valuation, Risks, And Key Takeaways

At current share prices near $26, Chewy trades at a market cap of $10.80 billion. Netting off the $878.8 million of cash (against zero debt) on Chewy's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $9.92 billion.

For FY26, Chewy is guiding to $13.60-$13.75 billion in revenue, which reflects 8-9% y/y growth (the midpoint suggesting slight acceleration from Q1 results, which is a fairly bullish indicator for Chewy). It's also expecting 6.7% in midpoint adjusted EBITDA margins, which reflects a full 100 bps of y/y margin accretion to $916.2 million at the midpoint (+27% y/y).

We note that in April, shortly after Chewy announced Q4 earnings, the company revealed its intention to buy a vet care practice network called Modern Animal, which adds 29 new practices to Chewy's network. Expected to close in the second half of FY26, Modern Animal adds $125 million in annual run-rate revenue and is expected to be EBITDA-neutral in the first year (but this contribution is not yet reflected in Chewy's guidance).

Taking current guidance as-is, Chewy trades at 10.8x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA, and against Wall Street's current consensus of $1.62 in pro forma EPS, the stock sits at 16.0x FY26 P/E (14.7x ex-cash). To me, these are quite cheap multiples for a company growing adjusted EBITDA at a mid-20% pace, with stable revenue growth and a continued focus on higher-margin businesses such as healthcare and ads.

Of course, there are risks to keep in mind here. Chewy is intensively scaling its vet care business this year with Modern Animal, and a messy integration could lead to headwinds on adjusted EBITDA. We may also start to see the macro impact of pet household formation, which would impact Chewy's growth rates.

That said, we see more positives than negatives in this stock, especially after this year's sharp declines. Stay long here and "Buy" the dip.

data.title: Aurora Cannabis: Why I Like This Stock
data.description:
I have followed Aurora Cannabis Inc. (ACB) for quite a while, but I removed it from my Focus List at 420 Investor in early 2024, as the company was failing in Canadian adult-use cannabis. While I haven't ever written about the company here at Seeking Alpha, I have followed it closely since it went public in 2014. I added it back to my Focus List in early February after it reported its Q3 and have a very large position in my model portfolio. In this article, I explain what has been going on with Aurora Cannabis and why I like it.

How Aurora Cannabis Has Transformed Itself

As I discuss in the look at the chart below, the stock of Aurora Cannabis has plunged from where it peaked in 2018 and trades at a tiny fraction of where it used to trade (down over 99%) and well below its IPO price. Still based in Alberta, the company suffered greatly after adult-use legalization was introduced in Canada in 2018.

Current CEO Miguel Martin joined the company in 2020 as Chief Commercial Officer from a CBD Wellness company he was leading from late 2018. Prior to this, he worked at Altria. In FY2021 ending in June, his first year as CEO, Aurora Cannabis had revenue of C$245.3 million, and it fell to C$221.3 million in FY22. Projected revenue for FY26, which ended in March, was C$361.7 million, which is up 5% from FY25, and shows that the company has grown during his tenure.

When it reported Q3 in early February, it announced that it is spinning off the Bevo division. The company announced the acquisition of Bevo, a supplier of propagated vegetables and ornamental plants, in the summer of 2022. It reported at that time that the company had recently had annual revenue of C$39 million with adjusted EBITDA of C$9 million.

Through FY26-Q3, total cannabis revenue was C$235.8 million, up 11% from a year earlier. Plant propagation revenue, which will be discontinued, was at C$46.8 million. The company never broke out profitability by segment, but total adjusted EBITDA for the first three quarters was C$44.7 million, an increase of 35%. So, Aurora has been expanding revenue and improving its margins. Net income has been negative for the year-to-date, but the company adjusts for inventory values, impairments and business transformation costs. Adjusted income for the year has grown 112%.

Looking at that cannabis revenue, the majority has been international, which has been C$127.7 million, up 33%. Aurora Cannabis operates in Europe, the UK, Australia and New Zealand. Canadian medical cannabis has grown 4% to C$83.8 million. The balance is in Canadian adult-use, which is very tiny relative to its overall revenue and to the market at just C$19.9 million (down 38%) and in wholesale at C$4.3 million.

While the company has declined substantially in the Canadian adult-use market as it has expanded internationally, one big change has been in the balance sheet, which now shows net cash. At the end of Q3, the company had C$61.8 million in borrowings and cash of C$56.4 million (excluding restricted cash) and short-term investments of C$52.1 million, giving it net cash of C$46.7 million. Of course, this strengthening of the balance sheet was due to heavy equity sales that have increased the share-count dramatically. Through the first three quarters of FY26, Aurora Cannabis operations consumed C$13.3 million in cash.

A Look Ahead

The company has not provided any updates on the spinoff of Bevo over the past 9 weeks, but the analysts presume that this transaction will occur, as they have reduced their sales outlook for FY27 to C$336 million, which is 7% below the outlook for FY26. Looking two years out, the FY28 estimate for revenue is 10% higher at C$369.6 million.

Analysts project adjusted EBITDA will rise 6% in FY27 and then increase 12% in FY28 to C$66.1 million. This is a margin of 17.9% of revenue. In FY26, the adjusted EBITDA is projected to have been C$55.6 million, which is 15.4% of projected revenue. While EPS are projected to be negative in FY27, analysts project that ACB will produce positive net income in FY28 of C$0.13 per share.

Aurora Cannabis Has a Very Cheap Stock

At C$4.67, ACB has a market cap of C$271.5 million. With net cash of C$46.7 million, the enterprise value is C$225 million. The price to tangible book works out to be a very low 0.6X, which is well below its peers. Looking at the enterprise value relative to the projected FY27 adjusted EBITDA, it trades at just 3.8X, which is much lower than peers.

I use the FY28 estimates to create a target a year out, and I think that the stock can get to 6X projected adjusted EBITDA for its enterprise value. This works out to be $397 million, which suggests a price for ACB of C$7.62, which at the current exchange rate is US$5.58. This is below the recent peaks but about 64% higher in price.

ACB has no strategic buyers currently, but the cheap stock could command some attention from prospective buyers. Even at $5.58, my target a year out, the stock trades slightly below tangible book value.

The Chart for ACB Is Encouraging

ACB is very beaten up, peaking at $1503 in 2018. The stock ran up a lot ahead of the rescheduling news that was rumored on Wednesday and took place on Thursday morning, trading above US$4, but then it plunged during trading on Thursday. It has lifted up slightly:

At $3.40, the stock is up almost 11% from its 52-week low (also an all-time low) set in March and down 19.4% year-to-date. This move is worse than the drop in cannabis stocks generally as measured by the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index, which closed at 5.51 on April 23rd, down 16.4% year-to-date. I currently see support in the $3 to $3.20 area, and I see several levels of resistance to the 150-day moving average, which is currently at $4.30. The company has already done more than one reverse-split, and this does not seem likely at this point given the distance from $1.

ACB is one of seven Canadian LPs in that Global Cannabis Stock Index, and it has performed poorly relative to its peers over the past few years:

One of the reasons I added it to my model portfolio at 420 Investor was its distance below the 150-day moving average, which it remains far below. Another reason I added it was that the 10-dma had been below the 50-dma, which had been below the 150-dma for more than 13 weeks, though that is no longer the case. I added to it on Thursday, and the size of the position is now about 19%.

Risks the Company Faces

Aurora Cannabis will be reporting its fiscal Q4 by the end of June, though it has not yet set a date. Quarterly results are often a risk for companies, but this one has an additional challenge of presenting its accounting post-Bevo. The things that stand out to me as being so low relative to the market cap or enterprise value, like tangible book value or projected adjusted EBITDA, may be lower than currently projected.

Cannabis sales in Canada's medical market are quite mature, but the market continues to grow overall. Relying on Canada for the majority of its sales introduces a potential risk. Of course, Aurora Cannabis is now in several foreign markets, and these are expected to grow sharply, but international sales can be risky due to potential regulatory changes.

Another risk is on the M&A front. The company has a better balance sheet now and can acquire companies with stock or with debt-funded cash. M&A can present risks, and one thing that Aurora might consider is entry into the U.S. NASDAQ still does not permit companies with U.S. operations to be listed, and two other companies that have U.S. assets have set up operations so that they hold the positions on their balance sheets rather than in their profit and loss. There is always the risk that the company overpays for a U.S. asset or that it gives up its NASDAQ listing in that process.

Conclusion

I see Aurora Cannabis as doing well with its business that is now going to be purely cannabis-focused and having an extremely inexpensive stock. The name is highly liquid, beaten up in price and very popular with Seeking Alpha readers given that it has 142K followers. My target a year out of US $5.58 is substantially higher.

data.title: XOP: Re-Rating Non-Conflict Oil Stocks
data.description:
Introduction

I believe the Iran war, which is currently at a standstill, threatens to keep over 10% of global crude locked in for far longer than envisioned, with eventual normalization occurring six months after flows begin. And it's likely that non-conflict oil will carry a premium; the world will seek energy security, and $10/bbl more is better than scarcity. At the same time, and far more important for oil stocks, is a re-rating from peak oil and overproduction to strategic assets with incredible cash flow that warrants far higher valuations, especially for non-conflict zones. Under this scenario, US oil companies should see significant earnings power and valuation boosts, and State Street SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Expl & Prod (XOP) should be the best vehicle for investors seeking diversification and exposure to many pure-play shale producers.

XOP Vs. XLE

Unlike the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), which is highly concentrated and includes oil service and pipeline companies, the XOP is closer to an equal-weight portfolio, with about 50% of the 40 stocks being junior shale oil producers. Ironically, the higher oil price may produce a surplus of natural gas (associated with oil production) that keeps the US domestic Henry Hub (HH) price capped vs. global LNG equivalents.

Portfolio Upside

One way to gauge an ETF's potential capital appreciation is to conduct a bottom-up analysis. Using consensus price targets for 95% of AUM, I calculated a weighted potential upside of 20% for the XOP, while for the XLE it is 10%. As can be expected, smaller or junior oil companies are far more sensitive to oil price changes than global integrated oil companies such as Chevron Corporation (CVX), which have refining, distribution, and pipeline operations, as well as assets in the Middle East. Refining companies such as Valero Energy Corporation (VLO) that I recently covered are in the middle in terms of oil price leverage, while domestic natural gas producers may be negatively impacted by overproduction of associated gas, while LNG exporters, such as Venture Global, Inc. (VG), may see windfall profits.

Higher Oil Price Impact

The main question I had was whether the consensus had incorporated the higher oil and LNG prices into estimates. As seen in the table below, yes, the majority of XOP holdings are forecast to see over 40% EBITDA growth in 2026, with some due to M&A and others, like PBF, coming from depressed levels. In addition, the market does not see a sharp reversal of oil prices, and in 2027, EBITDA is nearly flat.

Valuation

I have 25 years of experience covering oil stocks. Up to 2014, it was viewed as a core asset, with EV/EBITDA multiples in the 6-8x range, depending on reserve life, operating costs, and jurisdictional risk. That changed when OPEC decided to increase production, drive oil prices lower, and break the US shale sector. This created a permanent menace to cash flow and valuations, and investors de-rated the sector. This was further reinforced by decarbonization or electrification policies to reduce CO2 emissions, and finally, COVID provided another example of the frailty of oil markets.

Today, the Iran war has changed this; in my view, the world cannot operate without oil & gas and their derivatives, such as fuel and industrial products like fertilizers and helium. The center of the conflict provides 20% of global supply, and its lack of security has laid bare how dependent the global economy is on oil and on this region's output.

Thus, while many nations will accelerate energy security through nuclear and renewables, oil security and the strategic value of non-conflict zone producers should recover valuations, with high-growth companies garnering a premium.

The XOP consensus EV/EBITDA multiple is 4.5x 2026. Which, in my view, provides upside for a re-rating, especially in smaller companies with growth projects.

Risk

Oil prices may decline when the blockade is lifted, but I envision an $80/bbl range going forward. A greater risk is if the US government blocks oil exports, which would lead to a wider gap between WTI and Brent, hurt most XOP companies, and send a very negative signal to the sector. Other measures, such as windfall profit taxes and export taxes, designed to reduce the cost of domestic fuel and inflation, are also a risk to company profitability.

Conclusion

I rate XOP a buy. The US oil sector stands to benefit significantly from the near-term price dislocation caused by the Iran war, with cash flow expected to grow by 50% in 2026. Long term, the sector could re-rate as investors price non-confluent oil production at a premium. Finally, this event has demonstrated the world economy's frailty and dependence on the Gulf region and oil, and it should seek energy security that also rewards companies with production growth, as well as nuclear and renewable alternatives.

data.title: Vertiv: Backlog Silence Isn't Busting The Bull Case (Yet)
data.description:
Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT) has been on a rollercoaster ride since the company reported decent Q1 earnings and upgraded its FY26 guidance.

First, the stock was down after the print. In my view, the selloff was mainly driven by two things. First, management didn't disclose the backlog in Q1, which is not particularly great news from a visibility perspective.

Second, Q1 revenue came in pretty much in line with expectations. At 48x forward earnings, the stock needs a stronger beat to impress investors.

As I'm writing, the stock is up mid single digits from the lows on April 22. Which means that the market is looking past the backlog opacity.

Overall, I remain bullish on the stock, and I still own it in my portfolio. That said, I downgrade my conviction from a strong buy to a buy, as I want to see another guidance upgrade at the May investor conference.

Failing to see that upgrade, coupled with the backlog opacity, would make me downgrade further to a hold. In the meantime, I prefer to remain optimistic.

Q1 2026 Results: Good, But Not Impressive

Q1 2026 sales came in at $2.65 billion, up 30% YOY, with 22.6% organic growth. Even though Q1 revenue was $50 million above the midpoint of management's earlier guidance, the top-line results were pretty much in line with the Street's estimates of $2.64B for the quarter.

On the bottom line, adjusted operating profit increased to $550.9 million from $336.7 million, and adjusted operating margin expanded to 20.8% from 16.5%. In fact, the Q1 adjusted EPS came in at $1.17, beating the $1.00 consensus.

I have to say that Q1 earnings were not a big deal in terms of results. What caught my eye was the upgraded FY26 guidance (notice the red box that I highlighted below).

Essentially, management raised its forecasts for both top-line and bottom-line metrics. To be clear, I am not particularly surprised by the upgrade, given that the AI data center spending wave still looks intact. Among all 4 hyperscalers, the market expects at least $630 billion in CapEx spending this year.

From what I'm seeing, the Street wasn't particularly impressed either, given the limited upgrades in the revenue models for the next 4 quarters (compared to the jump in Q4 2025).

What indeed raised my eyebrow was the FCF flow through despite the increase in CapEx. Back in Q4, the company reported a full year adjusted FCF conversion of 115%. The Q1 2026 deck says the company remains on pace for about 90% adjusted free-cash-flow conversion for FY2026.

A quick look at the cash flow statement shows Q1 2026 operating cash flow at $766.8 million vs $303.3 million in Q1 2025. That's a 153% YOY increase.

In contrast, Q1 2026 capex rose from $36.5 million to $112.6 million, an increase of about 209%. So, even though CapEx is growing faster than operating cash flow, FCF is still expanding on an absolute basis.

Why is CapEx ticking up?

Well, as one would expect, CapEx is tied to growing capacity to support the current demand. During the last earnings call, CEO Giordano Albertazzi clarified where this investment is going:

These investments are strategic and positions us to meet the accelerating demand. We do this for growth, but also to bolster our overall operational resiliency. This capacity expansion is broad-based, power management, thermal management, infrastructure solutions and IT systems across all technologies.

In fact, earlier in March, the company put out a few PRs, where it announced "four new or expanding manufacturing facilities in the Americas."

From that PR, it seems that two South Carolina sites are expected to increase regional infrastructure capacity by about 7x when fully ramped, and a Mexicali expansion is expected to add about 45% regional capacity for power technologies.

Additionally, the company also announced a $50 million plan to expand its manufacturing presence in Ironton, Ohio.

So, the CapEx hike makes sense, and, unlike in the case of hyperscalers, it's not pressuring FCFs in a meaningful way, given the jump in operating cash flow. To be crystal clear, this is one of the main reasons why I prefer the picks and shovels to the hyperscalers.

Risks

The fact that the stock was down after reporting decent earnings and great guidance is a yellow flag to me.

It's yellow and not red because there was one development that I didn't particularly like in Q1 (and I believe it contributed to the drop post earnings): the fact that Vertiv did not provide a quarterly backlog figure.

For reference, in the last 10-K, the company disclosed a combined order backlog of $15.0 billion (vs $7.2B at the end of 2024).

What makes me sweat is the fact that the company has another important event just a few weeks away:

As noted above, the company will provide a financial overview during the investor conference. The risk is that the backlog may not be included in the overview. Therefore, the market may not sit well with that opacity, and I wouldn't be surprised to see another similar drop.

During the last earnings call, the CEO noted during the Q&A that the backlog may be "a little bit more elongated:"

What's in the backlog? Could you think about a backlog shape that is, if anything, a little bit more elongated, but not something dramatic to the point that the shape of the backlog is totally different. So there is no distortion of the backlog. If anything, it's a backlog that is a little bit more elongated. That, of course, gives us visibility, good visibility in 2027.

My concern here is that the "elongation" may be due to rumored delays in the AI data center buildout of major hyperscalers, including OpenAI (OPENAI) or Microsoft (MSFT).

If my concern materializes later during the year, the stock may see a drop as revenue recognition gets pushed to the right.

So far, the Street expects an acceleration in the revenue growth rate in the next 3 consecutive quarters.

Any downward revisions to the expectations above due to any delays in the buildout are a key risk to consider.

Valuation

The stock is trading at 48x forward earnings. In comparison, the median of the industrials sector is just 20x.

There is no doubt that the stock trades at a premium. In fact, even compared with some of its peers, Vertiv screens as the most expensive name in the group.

For reference, I included Eaton and Schneider Electric, as I believe these stocks are its closest peers in electrical infrastructure and distribution, backup power, liquid cooling and services.

From a price action perspective, the chart looks stretched, with the share price above the 50 and 200 MA. In fact, as seen below, the stock was barely pressured by the tech rotation earlier this year or the Middle East conflict.

Therefore, I wouldn't be surprised to see a digestion phase over the next few months. This is the main reason I downgraded to a Buy, despite remaining bullish on the stock in the long run.

Looking Ahead

Even though management didn't disclose the backlog in Q1 2026, they noted during the call that the pipeline remained strong and that they still expect orders to be up YOY in 2026.

For a practitioner like myself, I take the lack of figures as a lack of conviction. That said, despite the backlog opacity, the fact that the full-year 2026 guide was raised gives me pause in my skepticism.

Therefore, my focus is on the May investor conference. If management upgrades both the top line and bottom line estimates for the year, I may upgrade my conviction on the stock. If it remains unchanged, I may even downgrade to a Hold.

data.title: 2 Closed-End Funds At Discounted Bargains
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Closed-end funds can be fairly unique investment wrappers in that the share price can trade wildly differently from the underlying net asset value per share. That creates attractive opportunities to pick up exposure in a fund at a discounted bargain price. Of course, CEFs can trade at discounts for an endless amount of time. So we don't simply look at those with the largest discount and believe they are necessarily the best buys—that can happen if they look like they are being targeted by activists, but those are more tactical trades. Instead, we look at relative discount levels compared to the historical discount levels.

This is then essentially a mean reversion play on expecting the market to eventually shift the fund back up to its narrower discount level. While that isn't always guaranteed to happen, it also helps provide some type of discount floor. Funds can always continue to trade at wider discounts, but when already at a deep discount, we've tended to observe that added pressures from even further discounting are limited.

With all that said, we are looking at two such opportunities today of funds that are trading at historically deep discount levels. That includes the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (ETG) and BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST).

#1 Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

  • 1-Year Z-score: -2.96.
  • Discount/Premium: -10.63%.
  • Distribution Yield: 7.15%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.12% (2.28% with leverage costs).
  • Leverage: 16.65%.
  • Managed Assets: $2.22 billion.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

ETG invests "primarily in global dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and seeks to distribute a high level of dividend income that qualifies for favorable federal income tax treatment."

This is a fund that primarily invests in equity positions but also includes a small sleeve of preferred/fixed income. That essentially makes a more leveraged hybrid fund. The equity sleeve currently takes a very dominant ~89% allocation. We also see several of the mega-cap tech names make up the fund's top ten holdings.

On the other hand, we see some of the "global" names represented as the fund's name would imply. The hybrid approach and ability to provide positioning from anywhere around the globe provide some added flexibility and diversification for investors in a single wrapper.

The fund has seen its discount widen substantially from where it was trading at our last update—which would push us back to believing that ETG is back on the 'buy' list. As we can see, the fund has traded at double-digit discount levels previously, but it has been relatively short-lived.

The longest discounted period was in 2023/2024 before it started to recover. That stems from the fact that the fund had cut its monthly distribution at that time. That made the fund drop from what was flirting with a premium to a discounted level of over 12%. That isn't all that uncommon, either, to happen when funds make cuts to their level distributions.

Since that cut, the fund did ramp it back up, and given the modest distribution rate, it looks quite sustainable at this time. The current NAV rate comes in at 6.39%—and thanks to the fund's deep discount currently, the distribution rate for the market price also comes in a bit higher at 7.15%.

Overall, I suspect that they could even be in a position to possibly increase their payout. However, it will require capital gains to fund, and with increased market volatility, the management and board may want to be a bit more cautious before getting too aggressive here. At the end of the day, even if they don't raise the payout but outearn the 6.39% NAV rate and expense ratio, we would see some share price appreciation.

#2 BlackRock Science and Technology Trust

  • 1-Year Z-score: -2.09.
  • Discount/Premium: -8.51%.
  • Distribution Yield: 7.19%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.11%.
  • Leverage: N/A.
  • Managed Assets: $1.59 billion.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

BST's investment objective is "providing income and total return through a combination of current income, current gains, and long-term capital appreciation." They will attempt to do this through investing "at least 80% of its total assets in equity securities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. science and technology companies in any market capitalization range, selected for their rapid and sustainable growth potential from the development, advancement, and use of science and/or technology, and/or potential to generate current income from advantageous dividend yields."

At one point, this was a covered call writing fund where they would write call options against individual underlying holdings. However, they eliminated the call writing overlay strategy of their policy in November 2025. Though some see the removal of the options writing strategy as a bit of a worry, given the strong returns historically, that strategy would have actually held the fund back.

Instead of collecting option premiums to help fund the monthly distribution, it will now require only capital gains from its underlying holdings. The current NAV rate comes in at 6.58%, with the distribution yield on the market price at 7.19%. This fund has never cut its payout since its inception, which is thanks to this period being such a strong one for equities.

In particular, the technology space has been incredibly hot during this period, helping to drive the capital gains required to cover the payout. Even further, we've seen some significant appreciation in the share price, as it launched at $20 a share. Though some may point out that it is still down from the ~$60 level it reached during 2020/2021. I definitely won't complain too much, as that period was driven largely by speculative names rising substantially. With that said, they take some very hefty positions, such as NVIDIA (NVDA) being over 10% of the fund alone and Broadcom at 7.32%.

This sort of concentration could give some investors pause, but on the flip side, it does not employ leverage. So on that front, it could help to balance out a bit of that risk relative to other equity-focused CEFs, which often do employ leverage through borrowings.

While we've seen the market recover from the recent lows, the fund's discount has not recovered. In fact, we are seeing the discount widen in a way that we typically like to appreciate. That comes from the NAV rising sharply and the share price actually not being able to keep up at the same pace. So we get a fund's portfolio that is performing incredibly well, but investors are not giving it enough credit via its share price.

Given the substantial discount here, keep this name as a "Buy" for us. Though it is only more appropriate for investors who can handle a bit more volatility, given its more concentrated technology exposure. That has certainly helped the fund out massively historically, and the outlook remains strong given the expectation for impressive earnings growth moving forward.

data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper: Good Dividend And Excellent Growth Prospects At A Bargain Price
data.description:
My Investing Philosophy

I like quality companies that pay rising dividends. I also like to find companies that fit these definitions that are out of favor and selling at a discount to their respective fair values.

I use multiple sources of analysis and expected growth rates as well as articles, opinions, and corporate data releases/presentations. But I prefer to discern from all that for myself what I really expect from a company and then plug my assumptions into my models to find what I believe to be the true fair value of a company based upon discounted future cash flows and my own required rate of return.

I like to add stocks of companies to my portfolio that I expect to have competitive advantages, good management, and strong balance sheets combined with a business model that has weathered multiple periods of economic and geopolitical turmoil only to emerge stronger, which demonstrates resilience.

I believe that Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) fits into that description and I will use this article to explain why.

Company Background

Keurig Dr Pepper has formed as a result of the merger between Dr Pepper Snapple (DPS) and Keurig Green Mountain in mid-2018. The company ranks as the third largest non-alcoholic beverage company in the U.S. behind PepsiCo (PEP) and Coca-Cola (KO). KDP has three reporting units: U.S. Refreshment Beverages, U.S. Coffee, and International. Its major brands include Dr Pepper, Snapple, Keurig, Sunkist, Canada Dry, 7UP, A&W Root Beer, Core, and Bai, among others.

KDP announced an acquisition of JDE Peet’s, a Dutch coffee company, for ~$18.4B. According to management, when the acquisition is completed and operations integrated, the company plans to spin off the combined Keurig and JDE Peet’s Coffee as a separate, standalone coffee company listed in the U.S. This is expected to allow the two distinct companies to focus on respective growth and capital allocation without the stress of vying for capital under one management.

The acquisition is designed to create a global coffee giant (probably by early 2027) with interesting cross-selling, distribution, and marketing advantages, especially once it's separated from the KDP parent. It will also create a refreshment drinks company focused on increasing market share and innovation, something that the former Dr Pepper Snapple did very well before the merger with Keurig.

Earnings

Q1 2026 results were lackluster due to higher costs and management’s commitment to holding down price increases, but KDP beat consensus expectations on both adjusted EPS (earnings per share) and revenue for the quarter. Year-over-year results showed the stress of higher costs. While revenue grew, EPS fell. However, it should be noted that KDP did beat consensus estimates on both revenue and adjusted EPS for the quarter.

But management expects to mediate operations to improve margins by the second half of 2026. The acquisition of JDE Peet's is not yet included in the results and is expected to make significant improvements to both revenue and EPS growth.

Growth Prospects

Guidance from management is for robust growth and continued market share gains. Organic revenue growth is forecast to increase by 4-6% and JDE Peet’s is expected to add an additional $8.5-8.7B for 2026. That's expected to lead to double-digit growth in EPS.

The overall growth grade from Seeking Alpha of B, is appropriate given the slower growth experienced in 2025. However, looking at the forward growth prospects, KDP is expected to be miles ahead of its peers in 2026.

The one concern that stands out from the above table is CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) for levered FCF (free cash flow) due to the added interest expense resulting from the acquisition. But I'm confident that KDP management can pay down debt and continue to raise the dividend by reducing share buybacks over the next few years.

I want to emphasize the growth in revenue and EPS, both year/over/year and forward, compared to the sector median. Both are extremely high by comparison. Of course, this assumes that the combined companies mesh well and that management is able to capture synergies. But even if the forward consensus results are slightly higher than what actually occurs, I believe the acquisition will prove to be a success and provide shareholders with market-beating potential appreciation.

Profitability

The margins (as shown in the table below) are outstanding. Returns on Equity, Capital, and Assets are merely average, especially after a difficult year in 2025 with cost pressures pulling them down. I expect improvements in all these metrics in 2026 and further improvement beyond that from the two separate entities after the split, primarily because management of each company will be able to focus on its core competencies and allocate assets without having to compete with each other. It should create a much more efficient outcome.

The year 2025 was a trying year for KDP shareholders, adding to woes accumulated over the last five years. The table below shows just how difficult holding KDP shares has been and demonstrates the lack of synergy between the soft drinks and coffee platforms under one roof. But if we look at the 10-year total return performance compared to peers, it tells a very different story.

Dr Pepper Snapple outperformed it primary competitors before the Keurig merger. I expect returns to improve dramatically once the split is finalized by next year and for each newly formed company to become more competitive relative to PepsiCo (PEP) and Coca Cola (KO).

Balance Sheet

KDP’s total debt to equity ratio was much better than either PEP or KO prior to the recent acquisition. That will change as the company used leverage (more debt) and issued more shares to pay JDE Peet’s shareholders cash for their shares. But I think that seeing the ratios of both its competitors as high as they are while remaining very profitable should help assuage some of the concern over the added debt load for KDP.

Assuming the improvements to EPS come to fruition, the acquisition is expected to be accretive in the first year, thus allaying fears of dilution.

Dividend

Since the company was formed through a merger and resulted in a special dividend of over $100/share in 2018, dividend grades are difficult to calculate, but the yield of 3.2% is decent and compares well to peers.

Based upon prior history of the original DPS company, the company has paid dividends (in one form or another) for 39 consecutive years and raised the dividend for the last five years at an average pace of 8.9% per year. I believe that rate of increase will slow somewhat to 7.5% per year as the company allocates more free cash flow to pay down debt.

As long as the company keeps raising its dividend by more than the long-term rate of inflation, as a retiree, I’m happy.

Valuation

I want to emphasize that I believe the market reaction to the JDE Peet’s acquisition has been overdone and provides savvy investors an opportunity to buy KDP shares at a significant discount. I also expect that when the coffee segment is spun off, the combined value of the two stocks will be greater than the current single entity pricing. As I stated earlier, I expect the growth of each of the new companies to improve when separated. However, I'm going to remain relatively conservative in my valuation estimates using 7.5% annual dividend growth and 10% growth in EPS annually over the next five years.

Let’s apply a couple of different valuation methods to see what KDP should be worth going forward. First, if I look at KDP through the lens of my DDM (Dividend Discount Model), I get a very favorable outcome of a $36.80 fair market value.

For the discount rate I use in this model, I substitute my required rate of return for the cost of capital. That personalizes the formula to help me identify companies that meet my criteria for the long term rather than a valuation that is subject to change with movements in interest rates. I also vary the discount rate from one industry to another to keep my expectations realistic (I expect higher rates of return from technology than utilities, for example). KDP is in the beverages industry, for which I require a 10% annual compound total annual return on investment. So, my formula looks like this: D/(M-X)=V or $0.92/(.10-.075)=$36.80.

Where:

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount Rate

X = Expected Future Growth of Dividend

The next step is to determine the average annual increase to the stock’s value as it returns to fair value. the Excel formula is: =((F/C)^(1/5))-1 ; or

=((36.80/29.22)^(1/5))-1 = 4.2%

Where F is the fair value and C is the current stock price.

Now we add the average annual valuation gain of 4.2% to the dividend yield of 3.2% and the expected growth in EPS of 10% to get a CAGR of 17.9%.

Another way to value KDP is to add the dividend, expected growth in earnings, and the expected growth from the multiple expansion. This last piece requires a formula to calculate the annual compound expected growth in the PE (Price to Earnings) from the current level to the historical average (reversion to the mean). Again, the Excel formula is: =((F/C)^(1/5))-1

Where:

F = Future PE ratio

C = Current PE ratio

5 = Number of years expected to revert to mean

or

=((18/12.54)^(1/5))-1= 7.5%

This means that if the PE ratio rises over the next five years to a reasonable level of 18 from the current level of 12.54, the average annual increase in value due to PE expansion will be 7.5%.

Then we add the current dividend of 3.2% and the expected earnings growth rate of 10% to the value increase of 7.5%, and we get an expected annual compound total rate of return equal to 20.7%.

The fair value under this method is $40.86. (PE of 18 multiplied by the forward EPS of $2.27).

Finally, one other way to calculate the expected annual total return is to estimate the future EPS five years out multiplied by the expected PE of 18 to determine a target price for the stock and then add the estimated cumulative dividends we expect to collect over the five-year period. That gives us the total expected future value of our investment. Then, of course, we use another formula to determine the average expected annual total return.

Future estimated EPS in five years is calculated by assuming the average dividend growth rate of 7.5% over five periods to give us an expected future dividend of $1.22 in year five and a cumulative total of $5.34 collected from dividends over the five-year period. Using the 10% expected growth rate for EPS we find the expected EPS in year five (I only use four years of growth because we are using the forward EPS rather than trailing) to be $3.02/share. Multiply that by the expected PE of 18 and we get a future price target of $54.38. Then we add the future price to the cumulative dividends for a total of $59.73. That amount minus the current price (our original investment) of $29.22 results in a total gain of $30.51, or about 15.4%.

To calculate the compound annual total return rate, we use the formula: =(($59.73/$26.50)^(1/5)-1, or 17.6%.

I like the first two results better, but even at 15.4%, the investment easily outpaces my minimum required rate of return, so I’ll take it.

Risks

The main risk I see is that related to the execution of the acquisition and subsequent spinoff of the global coffee business. There are a lot of moving parts and culture integration requiring all parties to cooperate fully in the shared strategy. Any lack of buy-in by executives in either party could create unnecessary problems. However, on paper at least, the concept makes a lot of sense as long as everyone plays their respective role in support of the project.

Another risk is how management divides up the added debt load between the two and how the successive management teams go about reducing their respective debt burdens over the next few years. I will be watching for progress in each year by all parties on this important issue.

Of course, there's always a risk of a sudden economic downturn or a rise in job losses that forces a significant number of consumers to switch to cheaper store brand alternatives. However, such stresses are usually temporary, and quality companies such as KDP should rebound from such setbacks strongly once the economic environment improves (and it always does).

Finally, the concern that has hampered many food and beverage companies recently (and likely will for the foreseeable future) is how GLP-1 weight loss drugs affect demand. As I have stated in previous articles, most notably about PepsiCo, I expect that most demand losses will be temporary as people reach their goal weights and then revert to diets similar to what they had prior to starting taking the medications. I'm a prime example, having been on Mounjaro, from Eli Lilly, since last August and having dropped 36 pounds so far. Now that I'm nearing my goal weight (nine more pounds to go), my appetite is reviving, and the weight loss is slowing but continuing despite my less than perfect eating habits. If I stay on the drug long term, I may need to eat more just to keep from losing too much. So, I discount this risk, as I doubt it will be a permanent headwind.

Conclusion

I have to agree with the quant rating on this one, as it appears to be looking at forward EPS estimates to support a Strong Buy recommendation. The shares were sold off in a knee-jerk reaction to the added debt and potential dilution to shareholders caused by the structure of the acquisition. I believe that once the EPS reports in the second half of 2026 show the deal to be accretive, investors will come back. The drop over the last year has been overdone, in my opinion. The worst should be behind us, and the current price should be viewed as a great buying opportunity as the soft drink segment of the company gets back to its roots after the planned split. The coffee segment will also have good potential, as I think the two companies will be able to thrive better when managed separately. The new coffee company will have a global footprint with better growth and cost reduction potential on its own.

With a dividend yield of 3.2% that I expect to rise annually faster than the rate of inflation and selling at a significant discount to my fair market value estimates, KDP fits into my portfolio nicely.

data.title: Nebius Q1 Preview: The Market Is Saying This Is An Outlier Among Outliers
data.description:
Performance assessment

I've had an initial bullish view on Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), but I have still missed chances to add to existing buy positions:

Elevator pitch

I am finally ready to nibble a bit more on the buys for Nebius Group ahead of the Q1 FY26 earnings release on 29 April '26 before the market open:

  1. Nebius is growing its business with large multi-billion dollar mega deals.
  2. NBIS is having no trouble securing capital, but it still needs to secure more funding for capex.
  3. NBIS needs to grow like an outlier to justify its valuation.
  4. NBIS stock looks like it is breaking out above monthly resistance.

In addition to the business model, I think the history and context on the management team for Nebius Group are very important to understand. I suggest reading my first note on the company for this.

Nebius is growing its business with large multibillion-dollar megadeals

It seems hard to imagine, but Nebius is confident of increasing their annualized run-rate revenue (ARR) by 6.4x this year from $1.25B to $8B:

I showed in my last article how about $4.3B of this ARR increase in FY26 is already accounted for in deals signed last year. The remainder, ~$3.7B, is expected to come from a broad base of other customers.

Now, on top of this, we have a new $27B deal win from Meta (META) over 5 years. Updating the TCV and ARR math, I get a run rate of $9.7B:

Now, the ARR from this new deal won't hit the FY26 financials, as that capacity is due to be delivered in FY27. So the company still needs to secure more contracts to add up its tally to $8B ARR this year. So in my view, the Q1 FY26 print on ARR will be a key number the market will be focusing on.

A GPU shortage is leading to pricing power

Operating in the GPU-as-a-service market, Nebius Group is well-set to benefit from GPU availability near all-time lows:

I expect some juicy pricing tailwinds to emerge from this shortage to add onto the already existing 50% expansion in average selling price (ASP) the company saw QoQ in Q4 FY25 for both newer and older generation GPUs:

quarter-on-quarter, the number of year-long contracts doubled. We also saw a 50% expansion in ASP, and we saw prices go up even on older generation GPUs.

- Chief Revenue Officer Marc Boroditsky in the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026 call.

So look out for bullish commentary on price hikes in the Q1 call as well.

NBIS is having no trouble securing capital, but it still needs to secure more funding for capex

To capture more of the market opportunity in a supply-constrained environment, Nebius Group is aggressively investing in capex. For FY26, it expects to spend $18B to expand its data center capacity from 2GW to 3GW:

In order to capture the large and growing opportunity that we see for the future, we plan to invest in CapEx in the range of $16 billion to $20 billion in 2026. We already have about 60% of the capital needed for this range from our balance sheet, existing operations and commitments.

- CFO Dado Alonso in the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

This year, after raising $4.3B in convertible debt and $2B from Nvidia (NVDA), I estimate that NBIS' total liquidity is about $10B:

Accounting for economic cash flows (my proxy is adjusted EBITDA but without adding back the real cost of stock-based compensation) less capex, I see a $17.2B outflow:

So there is still a $7.2B shortfall. Now I don't doubt that NBIS will have difficulty securing funding, but this is another key thing to monitor in the Q1 update.

NBIS needs to grow like an outlier to justify its valuation

For high-growth stocks, I like to do a reverse-DCF-style valuation that looks at what the implied growth CAGR is at current market prices. I have conducted this kind of analysis already for various companies:

  1. Rocket Lab (RKLB)
  2. Palantir (PLTR)
  3. Tesla (TSLA)
  4. ServiceNow (NOW).

If this kind of analysis is new for you, I encourage you to check out those examples. Anyway, the implied growth CAGR formula is:

Here are the key parameters, assumptions, and analysis conclusion:

At today's price, to earn a market-level return over the next 8 years, assuming the stock trades at a 8.11x EV/Revenue terminal multiple at exit, NBIS needs to deliver 44.75% revenue 8-yr CAGR or $10.3B annual Revenue after 8 years.

This seems like a tough ask, but a few outliers have achieved this kind of feat:

And the market consensus seems to be implying that Nebius can join this league of outliers, as it is projecting $45.75B in revenue in 8 years (2033). This represents a 74% CAGR from TTM revenues of $534M as of December '25:

Given that its ARR next year is expected to be almost $10B, I too think ~$46B in 8 years is doable for NBIS given the current growth track. But let's not forget that this is a very demanding growth ask. So a slower-than-expected growth trajectory is a real valuation risk. And one argument that advocates for caution is the fact that the company has a poor track record in meeting consensus expectations for revenues:

NBIS stock looks like it is breaking out above monthly resistance

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

NBIS Technical Analysis

In the context of a broader uptrend, NBIS looks like it is breaking out of a monthly resistance level with a bullish engulfing candle. I expect a sustained breakout to lead to further upside in the stock.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to large multi-billion dollar contract wins and a favorable pricing environment.
  • Valuation: Neutral, as the valuation growth ask is quite high, and although I lean toward thinking NBIS can achieve the required growth, I recognize that the risks of failure and the market's high expectations introduce risk to the stock.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish due to a monthly breakout above a key resistance level.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Buy
